comment_text.sn42.2:1.2: His verses at Thag 19.1 speak eloquently of the tension between the worldly and the spiritual life. | The name tālapuṭa is explained by the commentary as due to his face being the color of a fresh palmyra fruit (tāla). This, however, leaves the element puṭa (“pouch”) unexplained. Tālapuṭa is known in Jain texts as a kind of poison (Uttarādhyayana Sūtra 16, verse 13;  Triṣaṣṭiśalākāpuruṣa Caritra 1.2). Tāla can mean “yellow orpiment”, a kind of arsenic used for coloring (Thig 14.1:28.1), so tālapuṭa would mean “pouch of arsenic”. As to why someone would have such a name, the protagonist of Isibhāsiyāiṁ 10.13—a government minister (amacca) named Tetaliputta—recounts having survived tālapuṭa poisoning. His life was, it seems, a series of tragedies leading him to an elaborate series of failed suicide attempts. While his story is unlike Tālapuṭa’s, the rhetoric is curiously similar: both rely on repeated, plaintive rhetorical questions to express emotional torment; for Tālapuṭa it is “when will it be?”, for Tetaliputta “who will believe me?” I wonder whether these stories share some connection. Perhaps the similar-sounding names have become confused, or perhaps he became known for the poison he survived.

root_text.sn42.2:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā rājagahe viharati veḷuvane kalandakanivāpe.
root_text.sn42.2:1.2: Atha kho tālapuṭo naṭagāmaṇi yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho tālapuṭo naṭagāmaṇi bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn42.2:1.3: “sutaṁ metaṁ, bhante, pubbakānaṁ ācariyapācariyānaṁ naṭānaṁ bhāsamānānaṁ:
root_text.sn42.2:1.4: ‘yo so naṭo raṅgamajjhe samajjamajjhe saccālikena janaṁ hāseti rameti, so kāyassa bhedā paraṁ maraṇā pahāsānaṁ devānaṁ sahabyataṁ upapajjatī’ti.
root_text.sn42.2:1.6: “Alaṁ, gāmaṇi, tiṭṭhatetaṁ. Mā maṁ etaṁ pucchī”ti.
root_text.sn42.2:1.7: Dutiyampi kho tālapuṭo naṭagāmaṇi bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn42.2:1.8: “sutaṁ metaṁ, bhante, pubbakānaṁ ācariyapācariyānaṁ naṭānaṁ bhāsamānānaṁ:
root_text.sn42.2:1.9: ‘yo so naṭo raṅgamajjhe samajjamajjhe saccālikena janaṁ hāseti rameti, so kāyassa bhedā paraṁ maraṇā pahāsānaṁ devānaṁ sahabyataṁ upapajjatī’ti.
root_text.sn42.2:1.11: “Alaṁ, gāmaṇi, tiṭṭhatetaṁ. Mā maṁ etaṁ pucchī”ti.
root_text.sn42.2:1.12: Tatiyampi kho tālapuṭo naṭagāmaṇi bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn42.2:1.13: “sutaṁ metaṁ, bhante, pubbakānaṁ ācariyapācariyānaṁ naṭānaṁ bhāsamānānaṁ:
root_text.sn42.2:1.14: ‘yo so naṭo raṅgamajjhe samajjamajjhe saccālikena janaṁ hāseti rameti, so kāyassa bhedā paraṁ maraṇā pahāsānaṁ devānaṁ sahabyataṁ upapajjatī’ti.
root_text.sn42.2:2.5: Pubbe kho, gāmaṇi, sattā avītarāgā rāgabandhanabaddhā.
root_text.sn42.2:2.6: Tesaṁ naṭo raṅgamajjhe samajjamajjhe ye dhammā rajanīyā te upasaṁharati bhiyyoso mattāya.
root_text.sn42.2:2.7: Pubbe kho, gāmaṇi, sattā avītadosā dosabandhanabaddhā.
root_text.sn42.2:2.8: Tesaṁ naṭo raṅgamajjhe samajjamajjhe ye dhammā dosanīyā te upasaṁharati bhiyyoso mattāya.
root_text.sn42.2:2.9: Pubbe kho, gāmaṇi, sattā avītamohā mohabandhanabaddhā.
root_text.sn42.2:2.10: Tesaṁ naṭo raṅgamajjhe samajjamajjhe ye dhammā mohanīyā te upasaṁharati bhiyyoso mattāya.
root_text.sn42.2:2.11: So attanā matto pamatto pare madetvā pamādetvā kāyassa bhedā paraṁ maraṇā pahāso nāma nirayo tattha upapajjati.
root_text.sn42.2:2.13: ‘yo so naṭo raṅgamajjhe samajjamajjhe saccālikena janaṁ hāseti rameti, so kāyassa bhedā paraṁ maraṇā pahāsānaṁ devānaṁ sahabyataṁ upapajjatī’ti, sāssa hoti micchādiṭṭhi.
root_text.sn42.2:2.14: Micchādiṭṭhikassa kho panāhaṁ, gāmaṇi, purisapuggalassa dvinnaṁ gatīnaṁ aññataraṁ gatiṁ vadāmi—
root_text.sn42.2:3.1: Evaṁ vutte, tālapuṭo naṭagāmaṇi parodi assūni pavattesi.
root_text.sn42.2:3.3: ‘alaṁ, gāmaṇi, tiṭṭhatetaṁ; mā maṁ etaṁ pucchī’”ti.
root_text.sn42.2:3.4: “Nāhaṁ, bhante, etaṁ rodāmi yaṁ maṁ bhagavā evamāha;
root_text.sn42.2:3.5: api cāhaṁ, bhante, pubbakehi ācariyapācariyehi naṭehi dīgharattaṁ nikato vañcito paluddho:
root_text.sn42.2:3.6: ‘yo so naṭo raṅgamajjhe samajjamajjhe saccālikena janaṁ hāseti rameti so kāyassa bhedā paraṁ maraṇā pahāsānaṁ devānaṁ sahabyataṁ upapajjatī’”ti.
root_text.sn42.2:3.8: Seyyathāpi, bhante, nikkujjitaṁ vā ukkujjeyya, paṭicchannaṁ vā vivareyya, mūḷhassa vā maggaṁ ācikkheyya, andhakāre vā telapajjotaṁ dhāreyya: ‘cakkhumanto rūpāni dakkhantī’ti; evamevaṁ bhagavatā anekapariyāyena dhammo pakāsito.
root_text.sn42.2:3.9: Esāhaṁ, bhante, bhagavantaṁ saraṇaṁ gacchāmi dhammañca bhikkhusaṅghañca.
root_text.sn42.2:3.10: Labheyyāhaṁ, bhante, bhagavato santike pabbajjaṁ, labheyyaṁ upasampadan”ti.
root_text.sn42.2:3.11: Alattha kho tālapuṭo naṭagāmaṇi bhagavato santike pabbajjaṁ, alattha upasampadaṁ.

translation_text.sn42.2:1.1: At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground.
translation_text.sn42.2:1.2: Then Tālapuṭa the dancing chief came up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to the Buddha:
translation_text.sn42.2:1.3: “Sir, I have heard that the dancers of the past who were tutors of tutors said:
translation_text.sn42.2:1.4: ‘Suppose a dancer entertains and amuses people on a stage or at a festival with mixed truth and lies. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of laughing gods.’
translation_text.sn42.2:1.5: What does the Buddha say about this?”
translation_text.sn42.2:1.6: “Enough, chief, let it be. Don’t ask me that.”
translation_text.sn42.2:1.12: And for a third time Tālapuṭa said to the Buddha:
translation_text.sn42.2:1.13: “Sir, I have heard that the dancers of the past who were tutors of tutors said:
translation_text.sn42.2:1.14: ‘Suppose a dancer entertains and amuses people on a stage or at a festival with mixed truth and lies. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of laughing gods.’
translation_text.sn42.2:1.15: What does the Buddha say about this?”
translation_text.sn42.2:2.1: “Clearly, chief, I’m not getting through to you when I say:
translation_text.sn42.2:2.5: When sentient beings are still not free of greed, and are still bound by greed,
translation_text.sn42.2:2.6: a dancer in a stage or festival presents them with even more arousing things.
translation_text.sn42.2:2.7: When sentient beings are still not free of hate, and are still bound by hate,
translation_text.sn42.2:2.8: a dancer in a stage or festival presents them with even more hateful things.
translation_text.sn42.2:2.9: When sentient beings are still not free of delusion, and are still bound by delusion,
translation_text.sn42.2:2.10: a dancer in a stage or festival presents them with even more delusory things.
translation_text.sn42.2:2.11: And so, being heedless and negligent themselves, they’ve encouraged others to be heedless and negligent. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the hell called ‘Laughter’.
translation_text.sn42.2:2.12: But if you have such a view:
translation_text.sn42.2:2.13: ‘Suppose a dancer entertains and amuses people on a stage or at a festival with mixed truth and lies. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of laughing gods.’ This is your wrong view.
translation_text.sn42.2:2.14: An individual with wrong view is reborn in one of two places, I say:
translation_text.sn42.2:3.1: When he said this, Tālapuṭa cried and burst out in tears.
translation_text.sn42.2:3.2: “This is what I didn’t get through to you when I said:
translation_text.sn42.2:3.3: ‘Enough, chief, let it be. Don’t ask me that.’”
translation_text.sn42.2:3.4: “Sir, I’m not crying because of what the Buddha said.
translation_text.sn42.2:3.5: But sir, for a long time I’ve been cheated, tricked, and deceived by the dancers of the past who were tutors of tutors, who said:
translation_text.sn42.2:3.6: ‘Suppose a dancer entertains and amuses people on a stage or at a festival with mixed truth and lies. When their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in the company of laughing gods.’
translation_text.sn42.2:3.8: As if he were righting the overturned, or revealing the hidden, or pointing out the path to the lost, or lighting a lamp in the dark so people with clear eyes can see what’s there, the Buddha has made the teaching clear in many ways.
translation_text.sn42.2:3.9: I go for refuge to the Buddha, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha.
translation_text.sn42.2:3.10: Sir, may I receive the going forth, the ordination in the Buddha’s presence?”
translation_text.sn42.2:3.11: And Tālapuṭa the dancing chief received the going forth, the ordination in the Buddha’s presence.
translation_text.sn42.2:3.12: Not long after his ordination, Venerable Tālapuṭa

variant_text.sn42.2:2.1: na labhāmi → nālatthaṁ (sya-all, km, pts1ed, mr)
